multidisc_handling.notes: ES scans subdirectories and detects loose disc image files (.cue/.bin/.gdi) alongside .m3u files, causing double-detection. Move disc images to a sibling folder outside the system ROM folder (e.g. images/) and update .m3u with relative paths (..\images\GameName\disc.cue).
Same ES double-detection issue as RetroBat. Keep disc images in the images/ subdirectory and reference them with relative forward-slash paths from the system ROM folder root.
ES-DE does not recurse into subdirectories by default, reducing double-detection risk. Media stored in platform-level Downloaded_* directories rather than per-system subfolders.
LaunchBox handles multi-disc natively via its database. Disc 2+ are added as 'Additional Apps' on the primary game entry. No .m3u files required.

Pegasus is metadata-driven. Collections are described by metadata.pegasus.txt files, so repair work usually focuses on correct metadata entries and launch commands rather than EmulationStation-style gamelist.xml rules.
EmuDeck typically uses ES-DE plus Steam ROM Manager. Folder names and parser behavior may be driven by EmuDeck profiles, so verify the active parser before bulk moves.
Recalbox is EmulationStation-based, so .m3u files should be real files at the system root and loose disc images can create duplicate entries.
RetroPie uses EmulationStation and runcommand. Multi-disc .m3u support depends on the selected emulator/core.
Playnite is a PC game library manager with emulator profiles and metadata providers. Library state is metadata-driven rather than ROM-folder-driven.
Parser rules decide what becomes a Steam shortcut. Duplicate entries often come from parsers matching both playlists and raw disc files.
RomM is a web ROM manager with server-side scanning and metadata storage.
Daijisho uses Android storage access and platform/player configurations; parser behavior depends on platform settings.

psx.name: PlayStation
psx.notes: Disc images are typically .bin/.cue pairs or .chd (compressed). Multi-disc games use .m3u playlists. BIOS required — region-specific (scph5500=Japan, scph5501=USA, scph5502=Europe).

ps2.name: PlayStation 2
ps2.notes: ISO is the most common format. Multi-disc is extremely rare on PS2. BIOS required — must be dumped from a real console. PCSX2 is the only viable emulator.

ps3.name: PlayStation 3
ps3.notes: RPCS3 is the only viable emulator — no RetroArch core. Games are installed from .pkg files or loaded as folder-based installs. Computationally demanding.

psp.name: PlayStation Portable
psp.notes: ISO is the standard format; CSO is a compressed variant. No BIOS required. PPSSPP works standalone and as a RetroArch core.

dreamcast.name: Sega Dreamcast
dreamcast.notes: GDI is the most accurate rip format for GD-ROM games. CHD is a popular lossless compressed alternative. BIOS optional but recommended for better compatibility. Multi-disc is rare.

saturn.name: Sega Saturn
saturn.notes: Saturn emulation is demanding. Mednafen (Beetle Saturn) is most accurate. BIOS required and region-specific. Multi-disc titles include Panzer Dragoon Saga (4 discs).

nes.name: Nintendo Entertainment System / Famicom
nes.notes: .nes (iNES) is the universal format. .fds for Famicom Disk System. No BIOS required.

snes.name: Super Nintendo Entertainment System / Super Famicom
snes.notes: .sfc is the standard format; .smc has a 512-byte copier header. Special-chip games (SuperFX, SA-1) need accurate emulators like Mesen or bsnes.

n64.name: Nintendo 64
n64.notes: .z64 is the standard big-endian byte-swapped format. .v64 and .n64 are alternate byte orders most emulators auto-detect.

gamecube.name: Nintendo GameCube
gamecube.notes: Dolphin is the only emulator. RVZ is Dolphin's modern lossless compressed format — preferred over ISO or GCZ. IPL.bin BIOS optional but needed for authentic boot screen.

wii.name: Nintendo Wii
wii.notes: Shares Dolphin emulator with GameCube. RVZ preferred. WBFS is a Wii-specific compressed format. Some titles use dual-layer discs (8.5GB).

gba.name: Game Boy Advance
gba.notes: .gba is the only ROM format. BIOS optional but recommended for accurate audio and boot behavior. mGBA is the accuracy standard.

ds.name: Nintendo DS
ds.notes: BIOS optional but recommended for accuracy. melonDS is the most accurate modern emulator and supports DSi emulation.

genesis.name: Sega Genesis / Mega Drive
genesis.notes: .md and .bin are the most common formats. .smd has a 512-byte copier header. Genesis Plus GX also handles Sega CD, 32X, Master System, and Game Gear.

wiiu.name: Nintendo Wii U
wiiu.notes: Cemu is the primary emulator. Libraries may be disc images (.wud/.wux), extracted Loadiine-style folders with code/content/meta, or installed title folders.

3ds.name: Nintendo 3DS
3ds.notes: Azahar is the modern Citra-family recommendation. Encrypted dumps require keys or decryption outside the frontend.

gb.name: Game Boy / Game Boy Color
gb.notes: BIOS optional for most emulators but useful for accurate boot behavior. GB and GBC may be separate systems in some frontends.

segacd.name: Sega CD / Mega CD
segacd.notes: BIOS required and region-specific. BIN/CUE or CHD are preferred. Multi-disc games can use .m3u playlists when supported by the core.

sega32x.name: Sega 32X
sega32x.notes: PicoDrive is commonly used for 32X. Genesis Plus GX support depends on build/core capabilities.

sms.name: Sega Master System
sms.notes: BIOS optional for most games. Many frontends group SMS separately from Game Gear even though cores overlap.

xbox.name: Xbox
xbox.notes: xemu is the main emulator. Redump ISOs often need conversion to XISO format for use.

xbox360.name: Xbox 360
xbox360.notes: Xenia is the primary emulator. Compatibility varies heavily; some libraries use extracted .xex folders or Games on Demand containers.

switch.name: Nintendo Switch
switch.notes: Requires user-dumped keys and firmware for most setups. Do not store keys in rom-librarian user.json.

psvita.name: PlayStation Vita
psvita.notes: Vita3K is the primary emulator. Games are usually installed packages or extracted app folders rather than simple ROM files.

mame.name: Arcade (MAME)
mame.notes: Arcade sets are version-sensitive. Do not unzip MAME ROM sets by default. CHDs often live in a subfolder named after the parent ROM.

neogeo.name: Neo Geo
neogeo.notes: Keep neogeo.zip BIOS with the ROM set or in the BIOS path expected by the frontend/core. Do not extract arcade ZIPs.

turbografx.name: TurboGrafx-16 / PC Engine
turbografx.notes: HuCard games use .pce. CD games use BIN/CUE or CHD and require a System Card BIOS for CD support.

atari2600.name: Atari 2600
atari2600.notes: Simple cartridge ROM system. Stella is the standard emulator.

atari5200.name: Atari 5200
atari5200.notes: BIOS required. Often supported through A5200/A7800 cores or MAME.

atari7800.name: Atari 7800
atari7800.notes: BIOS optional in some cores. A7800 and MAME are common choices.

atari800.name: Atari 8-bit Computers
atari800.notes: Computer platform with disk, tape, cartridge, and executable formats. BIOS files improve compatibility.

atarilynx.name: Atari Lynx
atarilynx.notes: Mednafen/Beetle Lynx and Handy are common. BIOS may be required by some cores.

atarist.name: Atari ST
atarist.notes: Computer platform with TOS BIOS. Multi-disk software is common.

jaguar.name: Atari Jaguar
jaguar.notes: Virtual Jaguar is common, but compatibility varies. MAME can also emulate some titles.

jaguarcd.name: Atari Jaguar CD
jaguarcd.notes: Small library, disc-based. BIOS/support varies by emulator build.

3do.name: 3DO Interactive Multiplayer
3do.notes: Opera/4DO lineage. BIOS required. CHD or BIN/CUE preferred.

cdi.name: Philips CD-i
cdi.notes: Usually handled through MAME. BIOS and CHD layout are important.

intellivision.name: Intellivision
intellivision.notes: Requires EXEC/GROM BIOS files for accurate emulation.

vectrex.name: Vectrex
vectrex.notes: Vector display system. Overlay artwork may be managed separately by the frontend.

odyssey2.name: Magnavox Odyssey 2 / Philips Videopac
odyssey2.notes: BIOS required. Often listed as Videopac in European frontend themes.

channelf.name: Fairchild Channel F
channelf.notes: Best handled through MAME. BIOS files are usually required.

msx.name: MSX
msx.notes: Computer platform with cartridge, disk, and cassette formats. BIOS/machine databases matter.

msx2.name: MSX2
msx2.notes: MSX2 requires different BIOS/machine profiles than MSX1.

sg1000.name: Sega SG-1000
sg1000.notes: Early Sega cartridge system. Often grouped near Master System/Game Gear.

gamegear.name: Sega Game Gear
gamegear.notes: BIOS optional. Commonly supported by Sega 8-bit cores.

ngp.name: Neo Geo Pocket / Color
ngp.notes: Includes monochrome Neo Geo Pocket and Neo Geo Pocket Color formats.

wonderswan.name: WonderSwan / WonderSwan Color
wonderswan.notes: Bandai handheld line. Mednafen/Beetle WonderSwan is common.

virtualboy.name: Virtual Boy
virtualboy.notes: Mednafen Virtual Boy core is the common choice.

supergrafx.name: PC Engine SuperGrafx
supergrafx.notes: Small SuperGrafx library. Often handled separately from PC Engine/TurboGrafx-16.

pcenginecd.name: PC Engine CD / TurboGrafx-CD
pcenginecd.notes: CD titles require System Card BIOS. CHD or BIN/CUE are common.

amiga.name: Commodore Amiga
amiga.notes: Computer platform with many disk images per game. WHDLoad/LHA sets are common in frontends. Kickstart ROMs are required.

amigacd32.name: Amiga CD32
amigacd32.notes: CD-based Amiga. Requires CD32 Kickstart ROMs.

c64.name: Commodore 64
c64.notes: Computer platform with disk, tape, cartridge, and PRG formats. Multi-disk games are common.

zx-spectrum.name: ZX Spectrum
zx-spectrum.notes: Tape/snapshot/disk formats all appear in libraries.

amstradcpc.name: Amstrad CPC
amstradcpc.notes: Disk, tape, snapshot, and cartridge formats are common.

apple2.name: Apple II
apple2.notes: Computer disk formats vary. WOZ is common for preservation-quality images.

apple2gs.name: Apple IIGS
apple2gs.notes: Requires Apple IIGS ROM for many emulator setups.

dos.name: MS-DOS / PC DOS
dos.notes: Folder-based games are common. Launch scripts/configs matter more than extension scanning.

scummvm.name: ScummVM
scummvm.notes: Game folders are identified by ScummVM IDs. Frontends often use .scummvm launcher files.

pc98.name: NEC PC-98
pc98.notes: Japanese computer platform with hard disk and floppy image formats. BIOS/font files usually required.

x68000.name: Sharp X68000
x68000.notes: Japanese computer platform. Multi-disk games are common and BIOS files are required.

naomi.name: Sega NAOMI
naomi.notes: Arcade platform related to Dreamcast. BIOS and correct ROM/CHD layout are required.

atomiswave.name: Sammy Atomiswave
atomiswave.notes: Arcade platform supported well by Flycast. BIOS ZIP required.

model2.name: Sega Model 2
model2.notes: Sega arcade board. The classic Model 2 Emulator is Windows-only; MAME support varies by title.

model3.name: Sega Model 3
model3.notes: Supermodel is the specialized emulator. MAME support varies by game.

cps1.name: Capcom Play System
cps1.notes: Arcade ROM set version must match emulator/core. Keep ZIPs intact.

cps2.name: Capcom Play System II
cps2.notes: Arcade ROM set version must match emulator/core. Keep ZIPs intact.

cps3.name: Capcom Play System III
cps3.notes: Some sets use CHD data. Keep arcade ZIP/CHD layout intact.

j2me.name: Java ME / J2ME Mobile
j2me.notes: Phone games are usually JAR/JAD pairs. Screen resolution and device profile can matter.

flash.name: Adobe Flash
flash.notes: Flash libraries may be standalone SWFs or Flashpoint-style curated launch entries. Network dependencies and external assets can matter.

shockwave.name: Adobe Shockwave / Director
shockwave.notes: Usually preserved through Flashpoint-like launchers or legacy projector/browser environments.

html5.name: HTML5 / Web Game Archives
html5.notes: Often folder-based with assets. Launcher command and local web server behavior can matter more than file extension.

pico8.name: PICO-8
pico8.notes: Fantasy console. Official runtime is commercial; Fake-08 provides broad compatibility.

lowresnx.name: LowRes NX
lowresnx.notes: Fantasy console. Smaller library but useful for completeness.

arduboy.name: Arduboy
arduboy.notes: Microconsole/homebrew platform. .arduboy packages may contain metadata plus hex data.

palm.name: Palm OS
palm.notes: Apps/games may require a device ROM and installed PDB data files.

symbian.name: Symbian / N-Gage
symbian.notes: EKA2L1 supports Symbian/N-Gage. Device firmware/data requirements vary.

android.name: Android APK
android.notes: APK/XAPK/APKS plus OBB data. Launcher setup and data installation matter more than extension scanning.

ios.name: iOS IPA
ios.notes: Early iOS app compatibility is emerging. DRM/signing and asset layout can be blockers.

windows3x.name: Windows 3.x
windows3x.notes: Usually launched via DOSBox with a Windows 3.x install. Config and mounted folder layout are critical.

windows9x.name: Windows 95/98/ME
windows9x.notes: Often managed as Wine prefixes or VM installs. Installation media and patches may matter more than single-file launch targets.

macintosh.name: Classic Macintosh
macintosh.notes: Classic Mac games need emulator profiles, ROMs, disk images, and sometimes StuffIt archives.

openbor.name: OpenBOR
openbor.notes: Beat-em-up engine. Version compatibility between PAK and runtime can matter.

mugen.name: M.U.G.E.N
mugen.notes: Usually folder-based full games. IKEMEN GO can run many MUGEN-compatible projects.

ikemen.name: IKEMEN GO
ikemen.notes: Modern fighting-game engine/runtime. Libraries are typically folders or launch scripts.

easyrpg.name: RPG Maker 2000/2003 (EasyRPG)
easyrpg.notes: Folder-based RPG Maker 2000/2003 games. Detect by RPG_RT.ldb/RPG_RT.lmt rather than a single ROM file.

solarus.name: Solarus
solarus.notes: 2D action-RPG engine. Games may be folders or packaged quest files.

doom.name: Doom Engine
doom.notes: Source port ecosystem. IWAD/PWAD separation and launch arguments matter.

quake.name: Quake Engine
quake.notes: Source port ecosystem. Base game data folders and mods need launcher-specific handling.

build.name: Build Engine
build.notes: Duke3D/Blood/Shadow Warrior-style source ports. Game data file names and port compatibility matter.

love2d.name: LÖVE / Love2D
love2d.notes: Lua game framework. Runtime version can matter for older games.

godot.name: Godot Exports
godot.notes: Not an emulator target in the classic sense. Usually native executable plus PCK assets.

vpinball.name: Visual Pinball
vpinball.notes: Pinball tables may depend on VPinMAME ROM ZIPs, scripts, B2S backglass files, and media assets.

daphne.name: Daphne Laserdisc
daphne.notes: Laserdisc games use ROM ZIPs plus video/audio assets and framefiles.

singe.name: Singe Laserdisc
singe.notes: Singe games are script/video asset bundles, commonly launched by Hypseus Singe.

teknoparrot.name: TeknoParrot / Modern Arcade PC
teknoparrot.notes: Modern arcade PC loader. Profiles/config XML and game install folders matter more than ROM extensions.

steam.name: Steam Shortcuts / Native PC
steam.notes: Launcher ecosystem rather than emulator. Shortcuts and artwork/parser entries are the library-management focus.

lutris.name: Lutris
lutris.notes: Linux game launcher. Manages Wine prefixes, native games, and emulator launchers.

heroic.name: Heroic Games Launcher
heroic.notes: Launcher for Epic/GOG/Amazon libraries. Metadata and launch entries matter more than ROM files.

wine.name: Wine / Proton Prefixes
wine.notes: Compatibility layer. Prefix path, runner version, dependencies, and launch command are the key state.

duckstation.name: DuckStation
duckstation.notes: Current gold-standard PSX emulator. Fast, accurate, excellent compatibility. Supersedes ePSXe as the community recommendation.

pcsx2.name: PCSX2
pcsx2.notes: The only viable PS2 emulator. Modern Qt-based UI. Requires PS2 BIOS dumped from a real console.

rpcs3.name: RPCS3
rpcs3.notes: The only viable PS3 emulator. No RetroArch core. Computationally intensive — requires a strong modern CPU.

ppsspp.name: PPSSPP
ppsspp.notes: Excellent PSP emulator with near-perfect compatibility. Supports resolution upscaling.

flycast.name: Flycast
flycast.notes: The best Dreamcast emulator. Supports GDI, CDI, CHD formats. Replaced Reicast.

mednafen.name: Mednafen
mednafen.notes: Multi-system accuracy-focused emulator. Exposed via RetroArch as the Beetle core family (Beetle PSX HW, Beetle Saturn, etc).

kronos.name: Kronos
kronos.notes: Saturn emulator forked from Yabause. Good compatibility, supports an OpenGL renderer for upscaled graphics.

dolphin.name: Dolphin
dolphin.notes: The definitive GameCube/Wii emulator. Very high compatibility and performance. Supports widescreen hacks, HD texture packs, and online multiplayer.

mesen.name: Mesen
mesen.notes: Mesen2 is a highly accurate multi-system emulator covering NES, SNES, GB/GBC/GBA. Best accuracy choice for supported systems.

snes9x.name: Snes9x
snes9x.notes: Classic SNES emulator with very high compatibility. Lighter than bsnes/Mesen, a good choice on older hardware.

project64.name: Project64
project64.notes: Windows-only N64 standalone emulator. Long-standing community favorite. No RetroArch core.

mupen64plus.name: Mupen64Plus
mupen64plus.notes: Cross-platform N64 core. Best used via RetroArch as mupen64plus-next with GlideN64 or Angrylion renderer.

mgba.name: mGBA
mgba.notes: Accuracy-focused GBA/GB/GBC emulator. Strongly recommended over VisualBoyAdvance. Supports optional GBA BIOS for accurate behavior.

melonds.name: melonDS
melonds.notes: Modern DS emulator, more accurate than DeSmuME. Supports Wi-Fi emulation and DSi mode.

genesis-plus-gx.name: Genesis Plus GX
genesis-plus-gx.notes: RetroArch-only multi-system Sega core. Covers Genesis/MD, Sega CD, 32X, Master System, Game Gear. High accuracy.

cemu.name: Cemu
cemu.notes: Primary Wii U emulator. Supports WUD/WUX and extracted game folders. No RetroArch core.

azahar.name: Azahar
azahar.notes: Modern Nintendo 3DS emulator descended from Citra-family work. Use decrypted dumps or proper keys where required.

picodrive.name: PicoDrive
picodrive.notes: RetroArch core commonly used for 32X and Sega CD compatibility, especially where Genesis Plus GX is insufficient.

xemu.name: xemu
xemu.notes: Primary original Xbox emulator. Redump ISOs often need conversion to XISO. No RetroArch core.

xenia.name: Xenia
xenia.notes: Primary Xbox 360 emulator. Compatibility and renderer requirements vary significantly by title.

ryujinx.name: Ryujinx
ryujinx.notes: Nintendo Switch emulator. Requires user-managed keys and firmware; do not store keys in rom-librarian configs.

vita3k.name: Vita3K
vita3k.notes: Primary PlayStation Vita emulator. Libraries are often installed packages or extracted app folders.

mame.name: MAME
mame.notes: Accuracy-focused arcade emulator. ROM sets are version-sensitive; keep ZIPs intact.

fbneo.name: FinalBurn Neo
fbneo.notes: Arcade and Neo Geo emulator with strong RetroArch support. Uses its own set versions; do not mix blindly with MAME sets.

stella.name: Stella
stella.notes: Standard Atari 2600 emulator. Available standalone and as a RetroArch core.

blastem.name: BlastEm
blastem.notes: Accuracy-focused Genesis/Mega Drive emulator. Also available as a RetroArch core in some builds.

a7800.name: A7800
a7800.notes: Atari 7800-focused emulator with useful Atari 5200/7800 coverage.

atari800.name: Atari800
atari800.notes: Atari 8-bit computer emulator. Handles disk, tape, cartridge, and executable formats.

handy.name: Handy
handy.notes: Atari Lynx emulator available as a RetroArch core.

hatari.name: Hatari
hatari.notes: Atari ST/STE/TT/Falcon emulator. Requires TOS ROMs for many setups.

virtualjaguar.name: Virtual Jaguar
virtualjaguar.notes: Atari Jaguar emulator with variable compatibility.

opera.name: Opera
opera.notes: RetroArch 3DO core descended from 4DO. BIOS required.

freeintv.name: FreeIntv
freeintv.notes: Intellivision RetroArch core. EXEC/GROM BIOS files required.

openmsx.name: openMSX
openmsx.notes: Full-featured MSX emulator with machine profiles and accurate hardware modeling.

bluemsx.name: blueMSX
bluemsx.notes: MSX-family emulator also used for ColecoVision in RetroArch.

ares.name: ares
ares.notes: Multi-system accuracy-focused emulator. Useful as a broad fallback/reference emulator.

vbam.name: VBA-M
vbam.notes: VisualBoyAdvance-M. Mostly a fallback now that mGBA is preferred for GB/GBA.

fs-uae.name: FS-UAE
fs-uae.notes: Amiga emulator based on UAE with good desktop launcher support. Kickstart ROMs required.

puae.name: PUAE
puae.notes: RetroArch Amiga core. Kickstart ROMs and WHDLoad conventions matter.

vice.name: VICE
vice.notes: Commodore emulator suite. C64 libraries may use disk, tape, cartridge, or PRG files.

fuse.name: Fuse
fuse.notes: ZX Spectrum emulator. Supports tape, snapshot, and disk formats.

dosbox-staging.name: DOSBox Staging
dosbox-staging.notes: Modern DOSBox fork. Folder layout, launch scripts, and configs are the key library concerns.

dosbox-pure.name: DOSBox Pure
dosbox-pure.notes: RetroArch DOS core designed for gamepad/frontend usage. ZIP-based DOS games are common.

scummvm.name: ScummVM
scummvm.notes: Adventure-game engine reimplementation. Uses game IDs and folders rather than conventional ROM files.

np2kai.name: Neko Project II kai
np2kai.notes: NEC PC-98 emulator. BIOS and font ROMs often required.

px68k.name: PX68k
px68k.notes: Sharp X68000 RetroArch core. BIOS files required.

freej2me.name: FreeJ2ME
freej2me.notes: Java ME mobile-game emulator. Device profile and resolution can matter.

kemulator.name: KEmulator
kemulator.notes: Legacy Windows Java ME emulator often used for older phone games.

ruffle.name: Ruffle
ruffle.notes: Flash Player emulator. Compatibility varies, especially for ActionScript 3 and network-dependent games.

flashpoint.name: Flashpoint Archive
flashpoint.notes: Curated web-game preservation launcher. Handles many Flash, Shockwave, Java applet, and web technologies through bundled launchers/proxies.

browser.name: Web Browser
browser.notes: Generic browser launch target for local HTML5 games. Some archives require a local web server.

fake08.name: Fake-08
fake08.notes: Open-source PICO-8 player with broad but not perfect compatibility.

cloudpilotemu.name: CloudpilotEmu
cloudpilotemu.notes: Palm OS emulator. Device ROMs may be required.

eka2l1.name: EKA2L1
eka2l1.notes: Symbian/N-Gage emulator. Device data and firmware requirements vary.

android-emulator.name: Android Emulator
android-emulator.notes: Generic Android emulator target. APK/XAPK/OBB installation flow matters.

touchhle.name: touchHLE
touchhle.notes: High-level emulator for early iPhone OS games/apps.

wine.name: Wine
wine.notes: Windows compatibility layer. Prefix state and runner version are critical deployment details.

sheepshaver.name: SheepShaver
sheepshaver.notes: Classic PowerPC Mac emulator. Requires compatible ROM and disk image setup.

basiliskii.name: Basilisk II
basiliskii.notes: Classic 68k Mac emulator. Requires compatible ROM and disk image setup.

openbor.name: OpenBOR
openbor.notes: Open Beats of Rage runtime. PAK/runtime version compatibility can matter.

mugen.name: M.U.G.E.N
mugen.notes: Legacy Windows fighting-game engine. Often launched through Wine on Linux.

ikemen-go.name: IKEMEN GO
ikemen-go.notes: Modern fighting-game engine compatible with many MUGEN assets.

easyrpg-player.name: EasyRPG Player
easyrpg-player.notes: RPG Maker 2000/2003 runtime. Detect by game folder contents.

gzdoom.name: GZDoom
gzdoom.notes: Doom-family source port. IWAD/PWAD launch arguments matter.

prboom-plus.name: PrBoom+
prboom-plus.notes: Doom source port with demo-compatibility focus.

raze.name: Raze
raze.notes: Build engine source port covering Duke 3D, Blood, Shadow Warrior, and related games.

eduke32.name: EDuke32
eduke32.notes: Duke Nukem 3D-focused Build engine source port.

godot.name: Godot Engine
godot.notes: Game engine runtime/editor. Exports usually launch as native apps rather than ROMs.

vpinball.name: Visual Pinball
vpinball.notes: Visual Pinball engine. Tables may require VPinMAME ROMs and sidecar assets.

hypseus-singe.name: Hypseus Singe
hypseus-singe.notes: Laserdisc emulator/runtime for Daphne and Singe games.

daphne.name: Daphne
daphne.notes: Legacy laserdisc emulator. Hypseus Singe is usually preferred now.

teknoparrot.name: TeknoParrot
teknoparrot.notes: Modern arcade PC loader. Profiles and install folders are key.

steam.name: Steam
steam.notes: PC launcher. Frontend integration usually means shortcuts, artwork, and parser metadata.

lutris.name: Lutris
lutris.notes: Linux game launcher and Wine-prefix manager.

skraper.name: Skraper
skraper.notes: Windows GUI scraper, free with a ScreenScraper account. Very popular for RetroBat and Batocera setups.

arrm.name: ARRM (Another Recalbox Romset Manager)
arrm.notes: Feature-rich Windows scraper and ROM manager. Supports multiple sources, ROM renaming, and media downloading.

sscraper.name: sselph/scraper
sscraper.notes: Lightweight CLI scraper. Fast and good for automated or headless setups.

launchbox-scraper.name: LaunchBox Built-in Scraper
launchbox-scraper.notes: Integrated into LaunchBox. Uses the LaunchBox Games Database. Some features require LaunchBox Premium.

skyscraper.name: Skyscraper
skyscraper.notes: Powerful CLI scraper with local caching. Preferred for Batocera and headless Linux setups. Supports Pegasus metadata format.

es-loose-images.description: EmulationStation scans subdirectories and detects loose disc image files (.cue, .bin, .gdi) alongside .m3u playlist files, causing each disc to appear as a separate game entry in addition to the .m3u entry.
es-loose-images.symptom: Games appear multiple times in the frontend — once for the .m3u and once per disc file. Multi-disc games show individual disc entries alongside the combined .m3u entry.
es-loose-images.fix: Move all disc image subfolders to a sibling directory outside the system ROM folder (e.g. sonyplaystation/images/ instead of sonyplaystation/Favorite/). Update .m3u files to use relative paths pointing to the new location (e.g. ..\images\GameName\disc.cue on Windows/RetroBat).

es-m3u-folder-name.description: EmulationStation-based frontends (RetroBat, Batocera, ES-DE) do not recognize a directory named with an .m3u extension (e.g. 'Game Title.m3u/') as a game. Only actual .m3u files at the system ROM folder root are detected.
es-m3u-folder-name.symptom: Games stored in folders named '<game>.m3u' do not appear in the frontend at all, even if the folder contains valid disc images and an inner .m3u file.
es-m3u-folder-name.fix: Rename the directory to remove the .m3u extension. Create a proper .m3u playlist file at the system ROM folder root referencing the disc images with correct relative paths.

7z-stdin-multidisc.description: When invoking 7-Zip inside a bash while-read loop that reads from a file (done < file), 7-Zip reads from and consumes the loop's stdin, causing all subsequent loop iterations to receive empty input. Only the first archive is extracted.
7z-stdin-multidisc.symptom: After running an extraction loop over a list of archives, only the first archive (Disc 1) is extracted for every multi-disc game. The loop processes fewer items than expected and exits early.
7z-stdin-multidisc.fix: Redirect stdin to /dev/null on every 7-Zip invocation inside loops: '"$SEVENZIP" e "$archive" -o"$dest" -y < /dev/null'

wsl-path-7zip.description: Windows 7-Zip (7z.exe) cannot handle Linux/WSL-style paths (/mnt/g/...). It requires native Windows paths with drive letters and backslashes (G:\games\...).
wsl-path-7zip.symptom: 7z.exe called from WSL bash with /mnt/... paths fails with 'cannot open file' or 'no such file' errors.
wsl-path-7zip.fix: Convert paths with wslpath before passing to 7z.exe: 'win_path=$(wslpath -w "$linux_path")'. Apply to both the archive path and the output destination path.

mv-same-name-dir.description: On Linux, 'mv source dest' moves source INSIDE dest (rather than renaming it) when dest already exists as a directory. This creates an unexpected nested structure.
mv-same-name-dir.symptom: 'mv "Game.m3u" "Game"' when a Game/ directory already exists creates Game/Game.m3u/ nested inside Game/, rather than renaming the folder. Subsequent writes to Game.m3u (as a file) may fail or produce unexpected results.
mv-same-name-dir.fix: Check for the existence of the destination directory before renaming: 'if [ -d "$dest" ]; then handle_conflict; else mv "$src" "$dest"; fi'

find-dir-name-match.description: The find command matches both files AND directories by name pattern. When directories are named with file extensions (e.g. 'Game.m3u/'), 'find . -name "*.m3u"' matches the directory itself in addition to any .m3u files inside.
find-dir-name-match.symptom: Scripts that search for .m3u files operate on directories instead of files, producing empty content reads, wrong path handling, or silent errors.
find-dir-name-match.fix: Always add '-type f' when targeting files: 'find "$dir" -type f -name "*.m3u"'. Add '-mindepth 1' as well when scanning inside a folder named with the same extension.

launchbox-native-multidisc.description: LaunchBox does not require .m3u playlists for multi-disc games. It tracks additional discs through its own game metadata and Additional Apps entries.
launchbox-native-multidisc.symptom: Imported .m3u files may appear as separate games or fail to map cleanly to LaunchBox's database-driven multi-disc model.
launchbox-native-multidisc.fix: Prefer LaunchBox's built-in multi-disc handling. Add Disc 2+ as Additional Apps or use LaunchBox import tools rather than converting every multi-disc set to .m3u.

mame-romset-version.description: MAME and FBNeo ROM sets are version-sensitive. A ZIP that works with one MAME or FBNeo version may be missing files for another version.
mame-romset-version.symptom: Arcade games fail to launch even though the ZIP exists. Logs mention missing ROMs, wrong checksums, missing BIOS, or missing parent sets.
mame-romset-version.fix: Identify the target emulator/core version, then audit against a matching full/non-merged/split ROM set. Do not unzip arcade ROMs as a repair step.

steam-rom-manager-duplicates.description: EmuDeck libraries may be surfaced both through ES-DE and Steam ROM Manager. Parser rules can create duplicate Steam entries if both playlist files and raw image files match a parser.
steam-rom-manager-duplicates.symptom: Steam shows duplicate games or individual disc entries after parsing, even when ES-DE looks correct.
steam-rom-manager-duplicates.fix: Check the active Steam ROM Manager parser and exclude hidden image folders or raw disc extensions when .m3u files are the intended launch targets.

switch-keys-secrets.description: Switch emulator setups require user-dumped keys and firmware, but these are sensitive deployment artifacts and should not be stored in rom-librarian user.json or committed to the repository.
switch-keys-secrets.symptom: Emulator reports missing prod.keys, title.keys, firmware, or encrypted content errors.
switch-keys-secrets.fix: Tell the user which files are missing and where the emulator expects them, but never ask the user to paste keys into chat or save keys in rom-librarian configs.
